results.prompt: What are the most important changes in the EU AI Act that take effect in 2025? Summarize with sources.
results.agent:
## Key EU AI Act Changes Taking Effect in 2025

The EU AI Act (Regulation 2024/1689) follows a staggered implementation timeline. Two major compliance deadlines fall in 2025:

---

### 1. February 2, 2025 — Prohibited Practices & AI Literacy

**Ban on "unacceptable risk" AI systems (Article 5).** Eight categories of AI practices are now prohibited, including:

- **Subliminal or manipulative AI** — systems that deploy techniques beyond a person's consciousness or purposefully deceptive techniques to push decisions causing significant harm
- **Social scoring** — systems that classify people based on social behavior or personal traits in ways detrimental to them
- **Emotion recognition** in workplaces and schools (with narrow exceptions for medical/safety purposes)
- **Real-time biometric identification** in public spaces (with narrow law-enforcement exceptions)
- Certain forms of predictive policing and exploitation of vulnerabilities

These prohibitions apply to both the development and the mere use of such systems, and have extraterritorial reach — companies outside the EU may still be subject to them.

**AI literacy requirement (Article 4).** All providers and deployers of AI systems must ensure their personnel have a sufficient level of AI literacy — the skills and knowledge to make informed decisions and use AI systems responsibly.

> *Penalties for violating prohibited practices: up to €35 million or 7% of global annual turnover (enforceable from August 2, 2025, when the penalties provisions take effect).*

**Sources:** [DLA Piper](https://www.dlapiper.com/insights/publications/ai-outlook/2025/eu-ai-acts-ban-on-prohibited-practices-takes-effect); [Jones Day](https://www.jonesday.com/en/insights/2025/02/eu-ai-act-first-rules-take-effect-on-prohibited-ai-systems); [Mayer Brown](https://www.mayerbrown.com/en/insights/publications/2025/01/eu-ai-act-ban-on-certain-ai-practices-and-requirements-for-ai-literacy-come-into-effect); [European Commission – AI Act page](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/regulatory-framework-ai)

---

### 2. August 2, 2025 — GPAI Obligations, Governance & Enforcement

This is the larger compliance milestone. Several major provisions become applicable:

#### a) General-Purpose AI (GPAI) model obligations (Chapter V)

**All GPAI model providers** must comply with baseline transparency obligations:

- Maintain **technical documentation** (model design, training process, data governance)
- Provide **downstream-provider information packs** so integrators can comply with the Act
- Comply with the **EU Copyright Directive**, including handling rights-reservation opt-outs
- Publish a **public summary of training data** content

**GPAI models with systemic risk** (trained with ≥ 10²⁵ FLOPS, or designated by the Commission) face additional obligations under Article 55:

- **Model evaluations and adversarial testing** (e.g., red-teaming, capability benchmarks, misuse-potential analysis)
- **Systemic risk assessment and mitigation**
- **Incident reporting** to the AI Office without undue delay
- **Adequate cybersecurity protection** for the model and its infrastructure

> *Penalties for GPAI violations: up to €15 million or 3% of global annual turnover.*

**Sources:** [European Commission – GPAI Obligations](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/factpages/general-purpose-ai-obligations-under-ai-act); [RAND – GPAI Models and Systemic Risk](https://www.rand.org/pubs/research_reports/RRA3243-1.html); [AI Act High-Level Summary](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/high-level-summary); [Agentic Fluxus – Article 55](https://agenticfluxus.com/blog/eu-ai-act-article-55-gpai-systemic-risk)

#### b) Governance structures become operational (Chapter VII)

- **National competent authorities** must be designated by Member States by this date, with contact details made public
- The **European AI Office** takes on its formal supervisory role for GPAI models
- **Notified bodies** (for conformity assessments of high-risk AI) become formally established

#### c) Penalties and enforcement provisions take effect (Articles 99, 100)

- Member States must have laid down rules for fines and penalties and notified them to the Commission
- National market surveillance authorities begin active supervision for GPAI and prohibited practices

#### d) Codes of Practice milestone (May 2, 2025)

By May 2, 2025, the AI Office was required to finalize **Codes of Practice** providing compliance guidance to GPAI model providers. If the Code was inadequate or not finalized by August 2, the Commission may issue implementing acts with common rules instead.

**Sources:** [AI Act Implementation Timeline](https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/implementation-timeline); [Greenberg Traurig](https://www.gtlaw.com/en/insights/2025/7/eu-ai-act-key-compliance-considerations-ahead-of-august-2025); [Lexara Advisory – Timeline](https://lexaraadvisory.com/eu-ai-act-timeline-us-organizations.html)

---

### Summary Table

| Date | What Takes Effect | Key Articles |
|---|---|---|
| **Feb 2, 2025** | Prohibited AI practices (8 categories) | Art. 5 |
| **Feb 2, 2025** | AI literacy obligations | Art. 4 |
| **May 2, 2025** | Codes of Practice for GPAI providers | Art. 56(9) |
| **Aug 2, 2025** | GPAI model transparency obligations | Art. 53 |
| **Aug 2, 2025** | GPAI systemic-risk obligations (adversarial testing, incident reporting, cybersecurity) | Art. 55 |
| **Aug 2, 2025** | Governance structures & national authorities operational | Ch. VII, Art. 70 |
| **Aug 2, 2025** | Penalties/fines provisions enforceable | Art. 99, 100 |

The next major deadline is **August 2, 2026**, when the full high-risk AI system requirements (technical documentation, risk management, human oversight, conformity assessments, CE marking) become mandatory.
results.claude:
I should flag up front that I can't verify live sources here, so treat the specifics below as my best understanding rather than citable fact — check the official text before relying on it.

The EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) entered into force on 1 August 2024 and applies in phases. The key milestones that take effect in 2025 are:

1. Prohibited practices (from 2 February 2025): The first set of obligations to apply are the outright bans on "unacceptable risk" AI. These include social scoring by public authorities, manipulative or subliminal techniques that cause harm, exploitation of vulnerabilities (age, disability, economic situation), untargeted scraping of facial images to build recognition databases, emotion recognition in workplaces and schools, certain biometric categorisation of sensitive traits, and most real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces by law enforcement (with narrow exceptions).

2. AI literacy obligation (from 2 February 2025): Providers and deployers must take measures to ensure a sufficient level of AI literacy among staff and others operating AI systems on their behalf.

3. General-purpose AI (GPAI) model rules (from 2 August 2025): Obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models begin to apply — including technical documentation, transparency to downstream providers, a policy to comply with EU copyright law, and a summary of training content. Models posing "systemic risk" face added duties (model evaluation, adversarial testing, incident reporting, cybersecurity). A voluntary GPAI Code of Practice was developed to help demonstrate compliance.

4. Governance and penalties (from 2 August 2025): The governance architecture (the AI Office, the AI Board, designation of national competent authorities) and the penalty provisions become applicable. Fines can reach up to 7% of global annual turnover (or EUR 35 million) for prohibited-practice violations, with lower tiers for other breaches.

Note on phasing beyond 2025: most high-risk system requirements apply later (broadly 2 August 2026, with certain product-embedded high-risk systems by 2 August 2027).

Suggested primary sources to confirm these details: the consolidated regulation on EUR-Lex (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689); the European Commission's "AI Act" pages and the EU AI Office; and the official Code of Practice for general-purpose AI. I'd verify the exact dated obligations against EUR-Lex, since the staggered timeline is where errors creep in.

results.prompt: Write a short cover letter for a data analyst role at a fintech. Candidate facts: 3 years SQL, dashboards in Tableau, A/B testing experience.
results.agent:
Here's a short, polished cover letter:

---

Dear Hiring Manager,

I'm writing to express my interest in the Data Analyst role at [Company Name]. With three years of hands-on experience in SQL, I've developed a strong ability to extract, clean, and model complex datasets — skills I'm eager to bring to a fast-moving fintech environment where data drives every decision.

In my current role, I build and maintain dashboards in Tableau that give stakeholders real-time visibility into key metrics, from user acquisition funnels to portfolio performance. I also design and analyze A/B tests end-to-end — formulating hypotheses, calculating sample sizes, monitoring results, and translating statistical findings into clear, actionable recommendations for product and marketing teams.

What draws me to [Company Name] is the chance to apply this analytical toolkit to financial products that have a tangible impact on people's lives. I thrive in environments where curiosity, rigor, and speed intersect, and I'd welcome the opportunity to contribute to your team's mission.

Thank you for your consideration. I look forward to the possibility of discussing how I can help [Company Name] make smarter, faster, data-informed decisions.

Best regards,  
[Your Name]

---

Let me know if you'd like me to adjust the tone, length, or tailor it toward a specific company.
results.claude:
Dear Hiring Manager,

I'm excited to apply for the Data Analyst position at your company. With three years of hands-on experience writing SQL to extract, transform, and analyze data, I'm confident I can help your team turn complex datasets into clear, actionable insight — something that feels especially valuable in a fast-moving fintech environment.

In my work, I've built interactive dashboards in Tableau that make key metrics accessible to both technical and non-technical stakeholders, helping teams make faster, better-informed decisions. I also have experience designing and analyzing A/B tests, which has taught me to pair statistical rigor with a focus on the questions that actually move the business forward.

I'd welcome the opportunity to bring this blend of technical skill and product curiosity to your team, and to contribute to data-driven decision-making in a domain where accuracy and trust matter. Thank you for your time and consideration — I'd be glad to discuss how I can help.

Sincerely,
[Your Name]

results.prompt:
Polish this email to a professor asking for a recommendation letter:
hey prof, i took your ML class last sem, got an A. applying to grad school, can you write me a rec letter? thanks
results.agent:
Here's a polished version:

---

**Subject:** Recommendation Letter Request — [Your Name], CS 4XX Machine Learning

Dear Professor [Last Name],

I hope this email finds you well.

I was a student in your Machine Learning course (CS 4XX) last semester and really enjoyed the class — particularly [mention something specific, e.g., the final project, a lecture topic, or an approach that resonated with you]. I was happy to earn an A, and your course deepened my interest in pursuing graduate studies in this field.

I'm now applying to [master's/PhD] programs for Fall [year], and I was wondering if you would feel comfortable writing a strong letter of recommendation on my behalf. I'd be happy to provide my CV, statement of purpose, a list of deadlines, and any other materials that would make the process easier for you.

Thank you for your time and for a great semester — I really appreciate your consideration.

Best,
[Your Full Name]
[Your Student ID / NetID]

---

A few notes on what I changed and why:

- **Subject line** — Clear and scannable; professors get a lot of email.
- **Specifics** — I left a placeholder for you to add something concrete about the class. This shows genuine engagement and helps the professor remember you.
- **"Strong letter"** — This phrasing gives them a graceful out if they don't know you well enough to write something meaningful.
- **Offer materials upfront** — Saves a back-and-forth and signals you're organized.
- **Tone** — Warm but professional; "hey prof" and "thanks" are too casual for this kind of request.

Would you like me to adjust the tone, length, or add any specific details about your grad school plans?
results.claude:
Subject: Request for a Letter of Recommendation

Dear Professor [Last Name],

I hope you're doing well. I took your Machine Learning course last semester and earned an A, and I really enjoyed and benefited from the class.

I'm now applying to graduate school, and I was wondering whether you would be willing to write me a letter of recommendation. I'd be happy to share my resume, statement of purpose, a list of programs and deadlines, and any other materials that would be helpful. Of course, I completely understand if you're unable to take this on.

Thank you very much for your time and for a great course.

Best regards,
[Your Full Name]
[Your Program / Student ID, if relevant]

glm_judge.why: A provides a more actionable template with helpful placeholders and explains the improvements, adding value beyond a simple rewrite.

results.prompt: Explain in 3 sentences why prompt engineering matters less than people think.
results.agent: Prompt engineering is often treated as a dark art, but most of what it does is compensate for models that aren't yet robust enough to infer intent from ordinary language—a gap that shrinks with every generation. The real leverage lies in the model's underlying capabilities, the quality of the data it was trained on, and the clarity of the task framing, not in arcane incantations or formatting tricks. As models improve at reasoning and instruction-following, the elaborate prompt-crafting that dominates today's discourse will look less like a skill and more like a temporary workaround.
results.claude: Prompt engineering matters less than people think because the biggest gains in output quality come from the underlying model's capability and the quality and relevance of the context you provide, not from clever phrasing or magic words. As models get better at understanding intent, they grow more robust to how a request is worded, so elaborate "tricks" that once helped tend to fade in value over time. What reliably matters is being clear about your actual goal, supplying the right information and constraints, and iterating on the result — which is mostly just good communication rather than a specialized skill.
